query: ideal depth Mineral Trioxide Aggregate (MTA) root end filling

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Materials
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramics, particularly MTA, can also be a core filling material. In addition to the aforementioned chemical properties of MTA, it is commonly used as an endodontic material due to its many beneficial characteristics. These include a long set time, a low relative compressive strength, high fracture resistance, and antibacterial and antifungal properties. [26] [27] Furthermore, MTA materials have been consistently proven in the literature to be biocompatible due to their high pH and ability to form hydroxyapatite, an organic substance that promotes bony growth. [28] There are several notable uses of MTA in endodontics, including in the repair of perforations during endodontic treatment, internal root resorption, apexogenesis, and pulp capping procedures. [11] [29]

retrieved_docs.content: Fast setting Similar microleakage than resin-modified glass ionomer cement Good marginal integrity Bond strength superior to MTA in furcation perforation repair Low porosity Insufficient radiopacity Biocompatible

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine is indicated as a material for furcation perforation repair. Furcation perforations are communications between the root canal system and the external dental surface in multirooted teeth. Repairing these perforations is a challenging aspect of dentistry. A study comparing two different dental repair materials, Biodentine and ProRoot MTA (Mineral Trioxide Aggregate), was conducted in dogs' teeth to treat furcation perforations. The key findings and conclusions from the study are as follows: [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Micro-CT Assessment: Biodentine had significantly less extruded material into the periodontal tissues than MTA. This is important because extruded material can hinder periodontal reattachment and tissue repair. The ability of Biodentine to minimize extrusion is seen as an advantage.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Cementum Repair: Biodentine showed significantly better cementum repair scores compared to MTA. This suggests that Biodentine may be more effective in promoting cementum formation in the furcation area.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic Assessment: Biodentine and MTA showed similar radiographic responses after 4 months. This means that the repair materials used have a similar appearance on radiographs of the treated teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine has emerged in recent years as an innovative bioactive dentine substitute. Its superior physical properties, improved handling, and various clinical applications have made biodentine popular in dentistry. It has been advocated for vital pulp therapy, root perforations, resorptions, retrograde fillings, and dentin replacement. Biodentine is calcium silicate-based cement formed by highly purified tricalcium silicate (80.1%). [1] Unlike mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA), it does not contain bismuth oxide, which is known to delay setting, affecting biocompatibility and causing discoloration. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Direct Pulp Capping
retrieved_docs.content: It's important to note that the choice of direct pulp capping material should consider various factors, including the type, size, and site of the pulp exposure and patient age. Although Biodentine appears promising, more research, especially with larger sample sizes, is needed to establish its clinical relevance and success rates compared to other materials like MTA and CH.

retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Absorption, if extruded past the apex Anti-inflammatory effects Antimicrobial effects [5] [9] Disadvantages: Transition into a weak, porous state upon setting Shrinkage upon setting [5] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Setting Reaction and Interaction with Dentin
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine stands out for its high levels of Ca 2+ release compared to other materials, such as calcium hydroxide (CH) cement, mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA), and resin-modified calcium silicate (TheraCal LC). [1] The material's solubility plays a significant role in this substantial Ca 2+ release. [1] Additionally, the release of OH − leads to an alkaline environment, supporting tissue repair and forming a protective necrotic zone between the pulp tissue and the capping material. [1] This necrotic zone safeguards the pulp cells from the alkaline pH of the material, fostering reparative dentin bridge development. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Histological Assessment: Biodentine and MTA demonstrated good biocompatibility, as there was reduced inflammation in the treated teeth, with only a few scattered inflammatory cells. Inflammation was significantly lower in the Biodentine group compared to the MTA group.
